content.title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC
content.summary:
UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease

A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential exposure to the Ebola virus.

The UK resident was medically evacuated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.

content.title: Healthy diet too expensive for one in three people globally, UN report finds
content.summary:
Global hunger declined for the third year in a row in 2025 but experts call for action to bring down costs of fruit, vegetables and dairy as report shows 2.69bn people can’t afford to eat well

One in three people cannot afford to eat a healthy diet, a new report has found, prompting calls for urgent action on food affordability.

There are 2.69 billion people unable to afford a diverse diet that meets their energy and nutrient needs, and it will take “immense” amounts of work to ac

content.title: São Tomé and Príncipe heads to polls in tense presidential election
content.summary:
Incumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independent

Voters in São Tomé and Príncipe go to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of Africa’s least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.

According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African state’s elections, approximatel

content.title: ‘We are preserving a tradition’: how Ghana’s sensationalist film posters became collectible art
content.summary:
Hand-painted works are often wildly unfaithful to the movies they portray – reinterpretations that sometimes resulted in threats, insults and even physical attacks from viewers who felt duped

Sitting on his porch in Teshie near Accra, Heavy J dipped a brush into red oil paint and dabbed it carefully on to his canvas – a flour sack – adding blood to a knife being wielded by a man. Higher on the canvas, he had started on an outline of a skull.

Heavy J was creating a poster, but not

content.title: Seven Americans quarantining at Kenya Ebola facility after US travel ban, says aid group
content.summary:
Aid workers are first known people to quarantine at facility, which sparked huge opposition in Kenya

Seven American aid workers who had been in Congo to fight the Ebola outbreak are quarantining at a new isolation facility in ⁠Kenya after the US government introduced travel ⁠restrictions, the head of a US charity ​employing them told Reuters.

The aid workers are the first known people to quarantine at the facility, which has sparked huge opposition in Kenya and is at the heart of

content.title: Canada cancels joint bridge-opening celebration with US amid trade tensions
content.summary:
Trump’s 50% tariff threat means there will be no ribbon-cutting celebration of Gordie Howe International Bridge

A planned joint US-Canada ceremony on Friday celebrating the opening of the Gordie Howe International Bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor, Ontario, is no longer expected to take place after Donald Trump announced a 50% tariff on most Canadian goods.

In announcing the tariffs, the president accused Canada of unfairly discriminating against US automobiles, alcohol and da

content.title: Nicaragua’s congress announces ‘work plan’ to suspend elections
content.summary:
US secretary of state says international community should ‘join forces’ against authoritarian Ortega regime

Nicaragua’s congress, controlled by the country’s long-serving president, Daniel Ortega, has announced a “work plan” to implement the authoritarian leader’s order that the country should no longer hold elections.

The UN and the US strongly condemned Sund

content.title: Sperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discover
content.summary:
Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close

Sperm whales produce a specific pattern of clicking noises when they hear the noise of boats, researchers have found. The pattern is so distinct that the scientists who identified it said they can now use it to predict when ships are nearby.

Researchers from

content.title: Trump administration signs commercial nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
content.summary: The agreement gives American companies priority access to nuclear reactors and fuel to Saudi Arabia. It's expected to last decades and be worth billions of dollars.

content.title: Why the U.S. and Saudi Arabia signed a nuclear agreement
content.summary: NPR has confirmed a deal that would allow the U.S. to transfer nuclear technology to Saudi Arabia and allow the country to begin enriching uranium for civilian use.

content.title: What to know about Ukraine's military shakeup
content.summary: After a week of nationwide protests over the direction of Ukraine's military strategy in the ongoing war with Russia, the Ukrainian military has a new commander, Mykhailo Drapatyi. Here's what to know.

content.title: Greetings from Jerusalem, whose holy sites host some of the oldest colonies of nesting swifts
content.summary: The scythe-winged birds have nested in the cracks of the Western Wall and other holy sites in Jerusalem for thousands of years.

content.title: Trump to attend dignified transfer of fallen soldiers. And, Hegseth testifies on Iran
content.summary: Trump will attend the dignified transfer of U.S. service members killed in the Middle East. And, Pete Hegseth is requesting billions from Congress to help with the rising cost of the war in Iran.

content.title: U.S. and Iranian attacks continue as tensions mount on a key waterway in the Red Sea
content.summary: The fighting comes as the Trump administration is seeking billions of dollars more from Congress for the war with Iran, which Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth says has already cost $37.5 billion.

content.title: 'It's all literally rubble': Lebanese poet returns to see what has become of her home
content.summary: A village destroyed. A home erased. One poet returns to remember what was lost.

content.title: Group of Indigenous Taiwanese row over 100 miles to recreate their ancestors' journey
content.summary: Indigenous people in Taiwan complete a journey to the Philippines in a traditional rowboat, recreating a journey their ancestors made thousands of years ago.

content.title: Ukraine's Zelenskyy fires top military commander amid ongoing protests
content.summary: Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelenskyy fires the country's army chief, just days after he dismissed the defense minister. Those two key figures had been feuding with each other.

content.title: French lawmakers approve a sweeping social media ban for children under 15
content.summary: The move makes France the first country in the European Union to pass a blanket ban on platforms as concerns grow worldwide over the harmful effects of digital content on kids.

content.title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
content.summary: The US Department of Energy says the "peaceful" co-operation agreement will give US firms "great access" to the Saudi nuclear energy programme.

content.title: Trump threatens to target Iran's bridges and power plants if Hormuz attacks persist
content.summary: Donald Trump says the US will respond any time Iranian forces shoot at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz.

content.title: Tankers make sharp U-turns after Houthi shipping threat
content.summary: All of the ships were travelling to or from Saudi ports before changing course, ship-tracking data shows.

content.title: Fire kills 10 members of same family in Peru, police say
content.summary:
Authorities are investigating the cause of the blaze in Lima as local media report
 it may have been arson.

content.title: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong
content.summary: Isabel Rose was convicted of trying to extort £100,000 from the UK banker and perverting the course of justice.

content.title: Wreckage of Pan Am plane that shaped aviation safety found 74 years on
content.summary: The deaths of 52 people on the Clipper Endeavor led to the introduction of mandatory pre-flight safety briefings.

content.title: Mamdani backs off pledge to arrest Netanyahu citing lack of authority
content.summary: The New York City mayor instead called on US authorities to act on International Criminal Court's arrest warrant against the Israeli prime minister.

content.title: Two Russians handed down jail terms for terrorism and spying in Angola
content.summary: The two, a political consultant and a translator, denied allegations of fomenting protests last year.

content.title: Ukrainian drones hit Russian online giant retailer Wildberries for second time
content.summary: Logistics hubs belonging to Wildberries in the Krasnodar and Stavropol regions were struck overnight.

content.title: Police formally investigate woman after Brit stabbed to death in French village
content.summary: Karen Carter was found with stab wounds in the Dordogne village she had lived in for over a decade.

content.summary: Trump administration says 'historic' agreement will 'reinforce global nonproliferation standards' and create jobs in US.

content.title: India’s Rahul Gandhi speaks out after release
content.summary: Indian opposition leader Rahul Gandhi has spoken out publicly for the first time since he was arrested during protests.

content.title: US regulators to weigh controversial peptides backed by RFK Jr
content.summary: FDA has questioned the evidence as demand surges for the compounds promoted for sleep, muscle growth, and anti-ageing.

content.summary: Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov is due to meet US counterpart Marco Rubio, but progress towards peace unlikely.

content.summary: UN rights chief condemns worsening repression in Nicaragua, urging the restoration of freedoms and the rule of law.

content.summary: US President Trump warned that Iran will ‘pay a big price’ right before he attended a dignified transfer ceremony.

content.title: Gaza man returns home after family believed he was dead
content.summary: Hamada Al Banna reunited with his family more than a year after an explosion that left him in a coma.

content.summary: Indian police deployed water cannons in Raipur at student protests demanding the resignation of the education minister.

content.summary: The newly appointed leader says Hamas would work to help the people of Gaza and achieve a united Palestinian state.

content.summary: Il presidente della Federcalcio argentina al centro di un'indagine per corruzione

content.title: Trump threatens Iranian infrastructure over Strait of Hormuz attacks
content.summary: US President Donald Trump warned on Wednesday that the US would destroy an Iranian bridge or power plant every time Tehran attacked shipping in the Strait of Hormuz. The threat came as Washington and Iran exchanged fresh strikes, regional missile alerts sounded and diplomatic efforts remained stalled.

content.title: France recorded 5,700 more deaths than usual during its historic June heat wave
content.summary: France recorded at least 5,700 more deaths than usual during a historic heatwave last month, the public health agency said Wednesday, sharply increasing its estimates of the feared toll from record-shattering temperatures. FRANCE 24's James Vasina reports.

content.title: US announces nuclear cooperation pact with Saudi Arabia
content.summary: The United States on Wednesday announced that it had reached a civilian nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, with Energy Secretary Chris Wright signing the pact and a separate bilateral safeguards deal alongside Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman. FRANCE 24's Fraser Jackson reports from Washington.

content.title: Congo's Ebola outbreak has killed over 1,000 people
content.summary: In tonight's edition, the Ebola outbreak in the DRC has crossed a devastating threshold, with more than 1,000 lives lost. Also, in Kenya, human rights groups warn that political “goons” are increasingly being used to disrupt rallies ahead of elections next year. And in Senegal, four years after devastating floods threatened the famous Pink Lake’s unique ecosystem, the lake is slowly regaining its iconic colour.

content.title: 'Mykhailo Fedorov was fired for his successes in fighting corruption in the defense sector'
content.summary: François Picard is pleased to welcome Daria Kaleniuk, Executive Director of the Anti-corruption Action Centre. Ukraine's wartime challenge is no longer only military, but institutional. Ms. Kaleniuk contends that battlefield success and democratic resilience are inseparable from deep reform of the defence establishment. While welcoming President Zelensky's replacement of the commander-in-chief with a younger, reform-oriented military leader, she argues that this represents only "half" of the nec

content.title: Sex trafficking in fashion: 'Outrageous that French legal system hasn't done anything by now'
content.summary: François Picard is pleased to welcome Dana Thomas, Paris-based journalist, specialising in fashion and culture, and author of "Deluxe: How Luxury Lost Its Luster". For decades, allegations of sexual exploitation in the fashion industry, spanning the globe, have resurfaced, often surrounding powerful figures whose deaths have complicated any semblance of accountability. In the wake of Daniel Siad's sudden death, Thomas offers a scathing indictment of the systemic failures within French law enforc

content.title: Exclusive: In Syria's Kurdish-majority city Kobane, postwar integration efforts get under way
content.summary: Northern Syria’s Kobane, or Ayn al-Arab, was a flashpoint city during the Kurdish fight against the Islamic State (IS) group nearly a decade ago. Today, it’s at the heart of the Syrian government’s bid to integrate the northern Kurdish majority region and their fighters. FRANCE 24’s Wassim Nasr had a rare opportunity to witness the integration process.

content.title: French modelling scout linked to Epstein found dead
content.summary: Daniel Siad, ⁠a modelling scout with ​ties to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, has been ​found dead at his home outside Paris, prosecutors said on Wednesday. Siad's body was discovered on Monday evening. An autopsy will be carried out ​to ‌determine the cause of death, the Nanterre prosecutor's ⁠office said. Siad's name appeared nearly 2,000 times in the Epstein files released by the ‌U.S. Department of Justice. FRANCE 24's Emerald Maxwell reports.

content.title: Drug gang threats force French police to put southern mayor under protection
content.summary: The mayor of Alès in southern France was taken from his home at night by an elite police unit amid fears of an imminent attack by the country’s most dreaded drug gang, officials said Tuesday. Christophe Rivenq had previously received death threats linked to the DZ Mafia gang, which has grown out of its Marseille base to become one of France’s main security threats.

content.title: 'Cockroach Janta Party' takes India by storm
content.summary:
Born from a satirical retort to a passing comment by India's top judge, the "Cockroach Janta Party" has grown into a full-fledged movement protesting the government's mishandling of the nationwide medical entrance examination in May.
 
Despite a violent crackdown on a peaceful march heading to the Indian parliament in New Delhi on Monday, members of the movement have kept up their protest.
 
FRANCE 24 explains how the CJP came to be.

content.title: Hungary: Ex-PM Orban pledges resistance to Magyar 'tyranny'
content.summary: Hungary's former prime minister alleges that his successor has "dismantled the rule of law and eliminated checks and balances." Peter Magyar has pursued Viktor Orban and his allies since winning April's elections.

content.title: Who could be the next UN Secretary-General?
content.summary: For the first time in a decade, the United Nations will soon choose a new Secretary-General. DW takes a look at the six candidates, what they stand for and the challenges they and the UN will face.

content.title: How Russia is luring foreign nationals to fight in Ukraine
content.summary: More and more, Russia is recruiting men from the world’s poorest countries to send them to the front in the Ukraine war. Why are people joining up?

content.title: Germany's growing interest in Africa: A win for both sides?
content.summary: Germany is deepening its footprint across Africa, as Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul embarks on his third trip to the continent in 2026. His top-level talks will focus on security, skills development and investment.

content.title: What's holding up the Gaza stabilization mission?
content.summary: A multinational stabilization mission is supposed to ensure the reconstruction of Gaza. Without political consensus among Israel, Hamas and the Arab states, its implementation remains uncertain.

content.title: Can India become the next semiconductor hub after Taiwan?
content.summary: India is investing billions to build a domestic semiconductor industry as major industrial powers seek to diversify supply chains beyond Taiwan.

content.title: Turkey-PKK talks: Is peace finally possible?
content.summary: A year ago, the Kurdish militant group PKK announced an end to its armed struggle. Since then, peace talks with Turkey have progressed slowly, but some observers believe they could succeed.

content.title: Jordan faces growing risk of becoming latest frontline state in US-Iran conflict
content.summary: As Iranian attacks intensify, Jordan is emerging as the latest frontline state in the widening Middle East conflict. Why is the Hashemite Kingdom a target for Tehran?

content.title: China narrows AI gap, challenges US influence
content.summary: At the World Artificial Intelligence Conference in Shanghai, Beijing showcased advances in AI while promoting its vision of open-source development and global governance.

content.title: How India's youth-led Cockroach movement challenges Modi
content.summary: What started as a satirical protest against exam leaks in India has grown into a wider movement voicing young Indians' anger over jobs and education.

content.title: Lunar Gateway: Earth’s guard post against asteroids?
content.summary: Humankind’s next space outpost, the lunar Gateway, will serve as a staging point to reach the surface of the Moon. A new ESA-backed study is considering whether it could also be used as a deployment point for planetary defence missions, to intercept asteroids approaching dangerously close to Earth.

content.title: ESA commissions world’s first space debris removal
content.summary: ClearSpace-1 will be the first space mission to remove an item of debris from orbit, planned for launch in 2025. The mission is being procured as a service contract with a startup-led commercial consortium, to help establish a new market for in-orbit servicing, as well as debris removal.

content.title: Hera’s radar CubeSat will peer into asteroid’s heart
content.summary: ESA’s proposed Hera asteroid mission for planetary defence will carry the smallest radar instrument ever flown in space – flying on a separate mini-spacecraft, it will approach two asteroids closer than its mothership and probe deep into their cores.

content.title: LG to Ban Residential Proxies from Smart TV Apps
content.summary: The home appliance giant LG Electronics USA said this week it plans to suspend any apps built for its smart TVs that turn one's television into an always-on residential proxy node. The move comes less than a month after researchers found that more than 42 percent of games and other apps available for download on LG's webOS store allow unknown third-parties to route their Internet traffic through a user's TV.

content.title: Microsoft Patches a Record 570 Security Flaws
content.summary: Microsoft Corp. today released software updates to plug at least 570 security holes in its Windows operating systems and other software, almost triple the number of vulnerabilities the software giant fixed in its record-smashing Patch Tuesday release last month. Microsoft attributed the burgeoning patch counts to vulnerability discoveries aided by artificial intelligence.

content.title: Lessons Learned from CISA’s Recent GitHub Leak
content.summary: The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) has issued a postmortem on a data leak in which a contractor published dozens of internal CISA credentials -- including AWS Govcloud keys -- in a public GitHub repository for almost six months before being notified by KrebsOnSecurity. Experts say the gaps identified in the agency's initial response provide important lessons that all security teams should absorb.

content.title: Felons, Fraudsters Flog Offensive Cybersecurity Startup
content.summary: A cybersecurity startup dangling millions of dollars to acquire zero-day security vulnerabilities in popular software is run by a pair of far-right conspiracy theorists and convicted felons whose most recent ventures included fake intelligence companies and a now-defunct AI-based lobbying platform they operated under assumed names.

content.title: FBI Seizes NetNut Proxy Platform, Popa Botnet
content.summary: The Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) said today it worked with industry partners to seize hundreds of domains associated with NetNut, a sprawling residential proxy service operated by the publicly-traded Israeli company Alarum Technologies [NASDAQ: ALAR]. The action comes roughly two weeks after KrebsOnSecurity published findings from multiple security firms connecting NetNut to the Popa botnet, a collection of at least two million devices that have been compromised by malicious software wi

content.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
content.summary: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

content.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
content.summary: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

content.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
content.summary: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

content.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
content.summary: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

content.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
content.summary: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

content.title: Global oil prices settle at 6-week high after topping $95 a barrel, as hopes dim for de-escalation of Iran war
content.summary: Oil prices settled at six-week highs on Wednesday after the U.S. struck Iran for the 11th night in a row and President Donald Trump warned that the U.S. will bomb Iranian bridges and power plants if Tehran fires on ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

content.title: ‘Owning a pet has become a luxury’ — vets worry about higher prices and shrinking pet population, survey finds
content.summary: A Morgan Stanley survey shows that U.S. vet practices are growing sales by raising prices as they contend with fewer visits —a formula that analysts say is unsustainable.

content.summary: Refinancing to a lower rate isn’t an option for many homeowners, who now face “financially unsustainable” mortgage payments.

content.title: The bull market faces higher likelihood of a Fed rate hike as Iran crisis intensifies
content.summary: Treasury yields are approaching the highest levels seen since the Iran war started in February as oil price rises and odds of a Fed rate hike narrow.

content.title: Commercial crude oil inventories increased by 2.0 million barrels
content.summary: For the week ending July 17, 2026, commercial crude oil inventories (excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve) increased 2.0 million barrels to 411.7 million barrels, 6% below the previous five-year (2021–2025) average. Gasoline inventories increased 0.8 million barrels, 7% below the five-year average. Distillate inventories increased 1.4 million barrels, 10% below the five-year average. Propane/propylene inventories increased 6.3 million barrels, 34% above the five-year average. Total commerci

content.title: New York imports more electricity from Canada after high-voltage transmission line opens
content.summary: On July 3, 2026, the New York Independent System Operator (NYISO) imported 52 gigawatthours (GWh) of electricity from Canada, the most traded between the two areas since January 2025. Some of the imported electricity flowed along the new Champlain Hudson Power Express (CHPE) transmission line between Quebec, Canada, and New York City, which officially reached commercial operations in May after three years of construction.

content.title: What are tank bottoms?
content.summary: Crude oil inventories held at storage facilities in Cushing, Oklahoma, fell below 20 million barrels during the week ending June 19 until the week ending July 10, according to our Weekly Petroleum Status Report.

content.title: Petroleum markets responded to disruptions in the Middle East in the second quarter
content.summary: Petroleum markets in the second quarter of 2026 (2Q26) were characterized by continued disruptions to international crude oil and petroleum product flows through the Strait of Hormuz, contributing to higher and more volatile crude oil prices through most of the quarter. The disruptions also resulted in international buyers seeking alternative supply sources for petroleum products, driving up U.S. refinery margins, production, and exports.

content.title: Global liquefied natural gas trade volumes reached record high in 2025
content.summary: Global liquefied natural gas (LNG) trade volumes increased 5.4% to a record 56.3 billion cubic feet per day last year (Bcf/d), driven largely by U.S. LNG export capacity expanding to meet growing demand, according to a recent report from the International Group of Liquefied Natural Gas Importers (GIIGNL). Global LNG trade has slowed this year following the closure of the key export route for Qatar, the world's second-largest LNG exporter.

content.title: The United States produced more crude oil than any other country in 2025
content.summary: The United States remained the world's largest crude oil producer in 2025, according to our International Energy Statistics database, extending a streak that began in 2018 when the United States overtook Russia to become the world's leading producer.

content.title: U.S. exports of crude oil and petroleum products reached record in April
content.summary: U.S. petroleum exports reached a record in April as disruptions to international crude oil and refined product flows through the Strait of Hormuz increased global demand for U.S. exports. Exports increased to 13.6 million barrels per day (b/d) in April, 15% more than the previous record set in March.

content.title: The 250-year history of U.S. energy consumption
content.summary: Over the 250-year history of our nation, energy consumption has evolved from wood use in the 18th and 19th centuries to today's use of modern renewable, hydrocarbon, and nuclear technology. In 2025, total energy used in the United States was 96 quadrillion British thermal units (quads), up 2% from 2024, but below 2007's record 99 quads. Petroleum was the most-used energy source last year, followed closely by natural gas. Use of renewable, coal, and nuclear energy each made up about 9% of total e

content.title: U.S. refining capacity decreased during 2025
content.summary: U.S. operable atmospheric distillation capacity, the primary measure of refinery capacity, totaled 18.2 million barrels per calendar day (b/cd) on January 1, 2026-down over 250,000 b/cd (about 1%) compared with January 1, 2025-according to our latest annual Refinery Capacity Report.

content.title: Metered electricity demand in the New York ISO falls midday because of small-scale solar
content.summary: An increase in electricity generation from small-scale solar in New York has decreased the midday demand for metered electricity, amid overall declining load in the state. The trend is particularly notable in the early spring (March and April), when solar generation has an outsized impact because demand is relatively low and conditions for solar generation are favorable.
